title: Making book cloth from fabric
subtitle: Laminating cotton fabric to Japanese tissue to stop adhesive strike-through
excerpt: Commercial book cloth is fabric backed with paper or acrylic to prevent the adhesive used in case binding from soaking through and marking the face. Making your own book cloth from a cotton remnant and thin Japanese tissue gives you control over colour, texture, and weight, and costs a fraction of the bought equivalent.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on preparing cover materials and linings.

glossaryTerms.definition: Woven fabric laminated to a paper or acrylic backing to prevent adhesive strike-through during case binding.
The visible mark left on the face of a fabric when adhesive applied to the back soaks through the weave.

content.text: uses Japanese tissue as the backing layer. Japanese tissue at 10-12 gsm is thin enough to add almost no stiffness to the cloth but dense enough to block
. A tightly-woven cotton (poplin, calico, or a plain quilting cotton) works better than loose weaves, which allow the paste to penetrate regardless of the tissue backing.

For the materials: tightly-woven cotton fabric (pre-washed to remove dressing), Japanese tissue at 10-12 gsm (available from specialist papermaking and bookbinding suppliers), and a smooth board to work on. A piece of glass or marble is ideal; a laminate board works. The wheat-paste should be freshly cooked and cooled to room temperature.
Cut the fabric and the Japanese tissue 20 mm larger in each direction than your finished cloth needs to be. The tissue will be trimmed to size after lamination.

Lay the fabric face down on the clean glass or board. Using a wide, soft brush, apply a thin even coat of wheat-paste to the back of the fabric. Work quickly; the paste should cover every area without pools or dry spots.

Lay the Japanese tissue over the pasted fabric. Smooth it from the centre outward with the flat of the hand or a clean piece of cartridge paper, working out any wrinkles. The tissue is thin enough that small wrinkles will flatten as it dries.
Lay a clean sheet of smooth paper over the tissue and press the whole assembly under a flat board with heavy books on top. Leave for 4-6 hours.

Once dry, peel the laminated cloth from the work surface. The tissue side is the adhesive face (the side you will paste in case-binding). The fabric face is the finished outside. Trim to size with the craft knife and ruler.

Before using it on a finished case, cut a small test piece and paste it to a scrap of binders board with PVA. Check for strike-through on the face: if PVA shows through the weave, the tissue is too light or the weave too open. In that case, apply two layers of tissue rather than one.

items.symptom: Wrinkles in the tissue that do not flatten
items.cause: The tissue was laid unevenly or the paste dried too quickly.
items.fix: Lightly dampen the tissue face with a mist of water, then re-press under weights.

items.symptom: The cloth is stiff and cardboard-like when dry
items.fix: A very thin paste coat is enough. The tissue does not need to be saturated. Use the paste as a thin, even film rather than a thick covering.
